description: VERVE-style hiring pipeline rubrics for ChamiNexT behavioral interviews and employer assessments.

stages.name: Online coding / Work Ticket
stages.chaminextMapping: Ship Test: small API endpoint, PR submit, AI allowed

phases.title: Ownership & big picture
phases.prompt: Tell me about a time you took ownership of a project that was failing or at risk. What was the broader context, what did you do, and what was the outcome?
focusAreas: Connects work to business or user impact
Takes initiative without being asked
Communicates status and risks proactively
Balances detail work with strategic awareness
strongSignals: Names stakeholders, constraints, and timeline pressure
Describes specific decisions they owned (not we)
Quantifies outcome or cites measurable improvement
Acknowledges what they would do differently
weakSignals: Vague team credit without personal contribution
Blames others without reflection
No connection to why the work mattered
Purely technical answer with no product or user lens
followUps: What would you have done if you had half the time?
Who disagreed with your approach and how did you handle it?
How did you know the project was actually back on track?

scoringDimensions.thinking: Judgment under ambiguity — did they see the real problem?
scoringDimensions.decomposition: STAR structure — situation, stakes, actions, results
scoringDimensions.codeQuality: Specificity — names, metrics, trade-offs vs generalities

phases.title: Pushback on No
phases.prompt: Describe a situation where you received a clear no from leadership or a stakeholder on something you believed was important. How did you respond?
focusAreas: Respects hierarchy while advocating with evidence
Separates ego from the idea
Proposes alternatives or smaller experiments
Knows when to commit after a decision
strongSignals: Presents data or user evidence, not just opinion
Offers a phased or reversible approach
Accepts final decision and executes fully
Maintains relationship with the decider
weakSignals: Persistent arguing without new information
Passive acceptance with visible resentment
Going around the decider without transparency
Framing the story as winning an argument
followUps: What evidence would have changed the outcome?
How did you keep the team aligned after the no?
Have you ever been glad you were overruled?

scoringDimensions.thinking: Picks battles wisely — impact vs politics
scoringDimensions.decomposition: Clear sequence: ask, present, adapt, commit
scoringDimensions.codeQuality: Concrete examples of what was proposed and why

phases.title: Force multiplier & mentorship
phases.prompt: Tell me about a time you helped someone on your team level up — or unblocked others so the whole team shipped faster.
focusAreas: Invests in others without neglecting own delivery
Documents, pairs, or reviews constructively
Scales knowledge beyond one-off heroics
Measures team outcome, not solo brilliance
strongSignals: Specific mentoring actions (pairing, docs, runbooks)
Junior or peer grew measurable skill or autonomy
Reduced bus factor or repeat incidents
Balanced helping with personal deadlines
weakSignals: Only story is doing everyone's work yourself
Vague we helped each other with no personal role
Mentorship as performance theater
No outcome for the mentee or team
followUps: How did you decide what to teach vs delegate?
What did you learn from the person you mentored?
When is it wrong to invest time in mentorship?

scoringDimensions.thinking: Systems view — team throughput over individual heroics
scoringDimensions.communication: Explains technical concepts to varied audiences
scoringDimensions.codeQuality: Artifacts left behind — docs, tests, patterns

phases.title: Pragmatism vs perfectionism
phases.prompt: Give an example where you deliberately shipped something imperfect to meet a deadline or learn faster. What did you cut and what did you protect?
focusAreas: Explicit trade-off framing
Protects safety, correctness, or user trust where it matters
Plans follow-up debt paydown
Avoids both yak-shaving and reckless shortcuts
strongSignals: Names what was deferred and why it was safe
Uses milestones or flags for incremental release
Monitors post-ship and iterates
Aligns with PM/stakeholders on scope
weakSignals: Perfectionism — endless refactor with no ship
Reckless shortcuts in security or data integrity
No awareness of technical debt created
Cannot articulate what good enough meant
followUps: What would you not cut even under pressure?
How did you communicate scope cuts to stakeholders?
Did the shortcut ever come back to bite you?

scoringDimensions.codeQuality: Technical choices that limited blast radius

phases.title: Candidate questions
phases.prompt: We're at the end — what questions do you have for me about the team, role, or how we build software here?
focusAreas: Questions show research and genuine interest
Probes team dynamics, expectations, and growth
Asks about trade-offs and how decisions get made
Avoids only comp/benefits in first pass
strongSignals: Asks about on-call, code review culture, or definition of done
Connects questions to their past experience
Asks what success looks like at 30/90 days
Follows up on interviewer answers thoughtfully
weakSignals: No questions prepared
Only generic what's-the-culture-like
Questions easily answered by the job post
Adversarial or entitlement tone
followUps: Given what you've heard so far, what concerns would you want to validate in a follow-up?
What would make you decline an offer?
What kind of manager helps you do your best work?

scoringDimensions.thinking: Curiosity and role fit — do they evaluate us too?
scoringDimensions.decomposition: Structured questions across team, product, process
scoringDimensions.communication: Active listening and building on answers
scoringDimensions.codeQuality: Depth — senior candidates ask about architecture and risks
